observation.summary: The note_second_category field (introduced for filename routing) is de-facto the sub-kind discriminator within multi-model environments, but was not originally designed for that purpose — whether to formalize it as the authoritative BB-subtype field, or introduce a separate bb_subtype: YAML field with closed enums per BB type, remains unresolved.

warrant.claim: The note_second_category field should be formally designated as the authoritative BB-subtype discriminator rather than introducing a separate bb_subtype field, because preserving an established de-facto convention minimizes ontological disruption while the level-boundary question remains unresolved.
warrant.data: note_second_category has already accrued a de-facto discriminator role across multi-model environments through actual usage patterns; introducing a parallel bb_subtype field with closed enums would create two competing subtype signals without a prior, independently established boundary argument to adjudicate which field occupies the authoritative meta-level position.
warrant.warrant: A meta-level principle—such as 'introduce a purpose-built field to achieve semantic clarity'—may legitimately adjudicate a schema dispute only when the level-boundary it presupposes has been established independently of the adjudication itself. Where that boundary remains contested or indeterminate (as it does here, since the very question of what constitutes the authoritative subtype axis is unresolved), the principle must be suspended and boundary-clarification must precede any structural change. Formalizing the existing field defers that boundary question without compounding it.
warrant.backing: Ontological conservatism is warranted precisely in reflexively isomorphic situations: when the field being redesigned is simultaneously an object within the ontology and a discriminator used to evaluate which ontology applies, any redesign risks co-constituting the very boundary it is meant to clarify. The meta-level warrant literature (cf. the provided warrant on frame separation) confirms that adequacy in such cases must be assessed by second-order tractability—the ontology's capacity to make the reflexive relationship manageable—not by first-order prescriptive elegance. Formalizing note_second_category preserves tractability by keeping the existing discriminative loop intact while deferring the harder boundary question.
warrant.qualifier: This position holds unless and until an argument for the note_second_category / bb_subtype boundary is produced that is logically independent of the subtype-discrimination problem itself; absent that independent argument, adding bb_subtype remains premature.

argument_a.evidence: Current multi-model routing behavior demonstrably depends on note_second_category as a subtype signal; no documented instance exists of a separately maintained bb_subtype field successfully resolving a routing or classification ambiguity that note_second_category could not have addressed, which undermines the empirical case for the added complexity.

warrant.claim: Before formalizing note_second_category as the authoritative BB-subtype field or introducing a dedicated bb_subtype field, the system must first establish a level-boundary that is logically independent of the routing and classification functions both candidate fields would serve — because adjudicating between them from within the domain they regulate is structurally disqualified.
warrant.data: note_second_category was introduced to solve a filename-routing problem and has since drifted into functioning as a sub-kind discriminator across multi-model environments. Two reform proposals exist: (A) ratify note_second_category in its current role, or (B) introduce a new bb_subtype field with closed enumerations per BB type. Both proposals use the same evaluative frame — operational fitness inside the multi-model pipeline — to assess a field whose value also governs how models within that pipeline interpret and route content.
warrant.warrant: A meta-level principle (here: 'the authoritative subtype field should be whichever best discriminates BB subtypes') is legitimately applicable only when the boundary between the evaluating frame and the object being evaluated is established prior to and independently of the adjudication. When note_second_category simultaneously participates in routing decisions and is proposed as the referent of those same routing decisions, condition (ii) from the available warrant fails: the case under adjudication is itself evidence that the level-boundary is contested. Consequently, neither proposal can be ratified by appeal to operational fitness alone without circularity. The boundary must be clarified first — specifically, a meta-level specification frame must be defined that is structurally distinct from the pipeline logic both fields are meant to govern.
warrant.backing: The reflexive-isomorphism warrant reinforces this: if the ontology that categorises BB subtypes is evaluated solely by the same processes it routes and classifies, no first-order fit criterion (descriptive frequency of note_second_category usage, or prescriptive coherence of a closed enum) can escape the co-constitutive loop. The adequacy criterion must shift to second-order tractability — does the chosen field design provide resources to distinguish its own constitutive role in routing from the phenomenal distinctions among BB subtypes it is meant to describe? That question cannot be answered from inside routing logic itself.
warrant.qualifier: This structural argument holds presumptively; it is defeasible if it can be shown that the routing and classification functions are already definitionally separated in existing documentation — i.e., that filename routing is governed by rules that are explicitly downstream of subtype identity, in which case note_second_category's drift would be a contingent rather than constitutive entanglement and option A might be ratifiable without circularity.

argument_b.evidence: The observed de-facto drift of note_second_category from routing label to sub-kind discriminator is itself the symptom: the same token is doing first-order routing work and second-order classificatory work simultaneously, which is precisely the collapsed level-boundary that the meta-level-boundary warrant requires to be re-established before adjudication can proceed. No current specification document is cited as having drawn this boundary independently, which sustains rather than refutes the claim.

contradicts.reason: Claim mismatch: A asserts 'The note_second_category field should be formally designated as the authoritative BB-subtype discriminator rather than introducing a separate bb_subtype field, because preserving an established de-facto convention minimizes ontological disruption while the level-boundary question remains unresolved.'; B asserts 'Before formalizing note_second_category as the authoritative BB-subtype field or introducing a dedicated bb_subtype field, the system must first establish a level-boundary that is logically independent of the routing and classification functions both candidate fields would serve — because adjudicating between them from within the domain they regulate is structurally disqualified.'

counter.counter_claim: A meta-level principle need not be established entirely independently of the domain it adjudicates; it is sufficient that the principle is grounded in recognized schema-design criteria (semantic clarity, single responsibility, explicit typing) that are themselves domain-neutral and widely accepted prior to this dispute.
counter.reason: The attacked warrant demands full logical independence of the level-boundary from the adjudication itself, but this sets an unattainable standard: virtually any governing principle is partly constituted by the distinctions it regulates. The warrant conflates 'the principle must not beg the question in favor of one candidate field' with 'the principle must be established with zero reference to the domain.' Schema-design principles like 'introduce a purpose-built field for semantic clarity' derive their authority from cross-domain software-engineering norms, not from the BB-subtype dispute itself. Therefore the boundary is not as indeterminate as the warrant claims; it is anchored by external criteria, and the requirement to suspend the principle until some prior boundary-clarification is complete is unsupported.

pattern.description: The contradiction reveals a structural regularity in which the independence requirement for meta-level adjudicating principles is context-sensitive rather than absolute: a principle qualifies as authoritative not by being wholly external to the domain, but by being grounded in higher-order, domain-neutral criteria that were accepted prior to and apart from the specific dispute. The broken warrant assumed full independence as a binary condition, while the counter-argument exposes that partial grounding in pre-established, cross-domain standards satisfies the functional role of neutrality. This pattern recurs whenever a system mistakes the origin-purity of a principle for its adjudicative legitimacy, overlooking that legitimacy can be inherited from more abstract, universally accepted criteria.
observed: meta-level authority through ancestry not externality

revised_warrant.claim: A meta-level principle possesses sufficient adjudicative authority to resolve domain-internal disputes
revised_warrant.data: The principle is grounded in higher-order, domain-neutral criteria that were explicitly accepted prior to and independently of the specific dispute under adjudication
revised_warrant.warrant: Adjudicative legitimacy is conferred not by a principle's complete external origin relative to the domain, but by its derivation from pre-established, cross-domain standards whose acceptance predates the dispute; a principle satisfies the neutrality requirement when its foundational criteria are domain-neutral and temporally prior, even if the principle itself has partial application within the domain, because the functional role of neutrality—preventing self-serving adjudication—is fulfilled by the independence of its grounding rather than the independence of its entire genealogy
revised_warrant.backing: Jurisprudential and philosophical precedent distinguishes procedural legitimacy from origin purity: constitutional principles gain authority from prior social contract acceptance rather than from standing wholly outside legal practice; logical axioms adjudicate mathematical disputes without being external to formal systems because their acceptance precedes any particular proof controversy; meta-ethical frameworks arbitrate moral disputes through pre-committed, abstract criteria rather than through complete separation from ethical discourse; in each case the locus of independence is the grounding criteria, not the principle's domain membership
revised_warrant.qualifier: This warrant holds provided that (1) the higher-order grounding criteria are genuinely domain-neutral and not merely relabeled domain-specific preferences, (2) prior acceptance of those criteria is demonstrable and was not strategically manufactured in anticipation of the dispute, and (3) the partial domain involvement of the principle does not create a systematic bias favoring one disputant; where any of these conditions fail, full external independence may be required
